skill.name: image-edit
skill.description: Routes image-edit requests to the appropriate RunComfy model, including batch edits, text rewrites, local edits, and mask-based inpainting. It provides model selection guidance and example RunComfy CLI commands for supported edit workflows.
skill.summary: Route image-edit tasks to RunComfy models with prompt and CLI guidance.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Static command findings are mostly false positives from Markdown code spans and fenced CLI examples, not embedded Ruby or shell automation. The real risk is moderate: the skill instructs agents to run a local RunComfy CLI that sends prompts and public image URLs to RunComfy services, stores an API token through the CLI, and downloads generated outputs. No evidence found of malicious intent or prompt-injection text.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Runs Remote Image-Edit Jobs
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs agents to install and run the RunComfy CLI, then submit JSON edit requests to remote model endpoints. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it executes an external tool and moves user prompts, image URLs, and generated results outside the local workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples and the How it works section clearly describe local CLI execution and remote API submission. The intent appears legitimate, so the risk is operational and privacy-related rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: User Image URLs Are Shared With A Remote Service
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The documented schemas require public HTTPS image or mask URLs that RunComfy model servers fetch for processing. Users may expose private or sensitive media if they provide confidential URLs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple schema sections require URL inputs, and the privacy section states that RunComfy model servers fetch those URLs. This directly supports a moderate data exposure warning.

low_findings.title: Documented Token Storage In User Config Directory
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE WITH LIMITED IMPACT: The skill notes that RunComfy login stores an API token under the user config directory with owner-only permissions. This is standard CLI credential storage, but users should understand that a persistent local credential is created unless RUNCOMFY_TOKEN is used.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line explicitly documents token storage and file permissions. The described 0600 mode lowers the impact, but credential persistence remains relevant.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Command Detections Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The numerous Ruby/shell backtick detections are caused by Markdown code spans, tables, and fenced command examples. I did not find executable Ruby code, hidden shell scripts, or dynamic command construction in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is Markdown and the cited locations are prose or fenced examples. There is no evidence that these backticks execute inside the skill package.

low_findings.title: Static Path Traversal And Weak Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The path traversal alerts map to placeholder HTTPS URLs and output directory placeholders, not filesystem traversal logic. The weak cryptography alerts do not correspond to cryptographic code or algorithm selection in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation examples, limitation text, exit-code headings, or token-path documentation. I did not find cryptographic operations or path traversal behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Networked CLI Invocation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill pattern combines local external command execution with remote model submission and output downloads. This is expected functionality, but marketplace users need a clear warning before publishing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines show concrete RunComfy CLI invocations and describe remote API requests, polling, downloads, and endpoint limits. No malicious callback or telemetry pattern was found.

content.value_statement: Choosing the right image-edit model is difficult when each edit needs different strengths. This skill routes requests to RunComfy models and gives focused prompts for reliable edits.
actual_capabilities: Selects a RunComfy edit model based on task intent.
Provides CLI examples for Nano Banana Edit, GPT Image 2 Edit, Flux Kontext Pro, and Z-Image Turbo Inpaint.
Supports guidance for batch edits, background swaps, object removal, and in-image text replacement.
Documents required input fields, optional settings, and model-specific limits.
Includes prompt patterns for preservation, spatial scope, multilingual text, and masks.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI, a RunComfy account, and authentication.
Uses remote RunComfy services, so image URLs and prompts leave the local workspace.
Requires publicly fetchable HTTPS URLs for source images and masks.
Selects one model per call and does not combine multiple routes automatically.

use_cases.title: Prepare Product Image Variants
use_cases.description: Batch edit product images with consistent aspect ratio, resolution, and background changes.

use_cases.title: Localize Campaign Creative
use_cases.description: Replace in-image headlines across languages while preserving layout, brand marks, and source photography.

use_cases.title: Retouch Specific Regions
use_cases.description: Use mask-driven inpainting to remove objects, clean backgrounds, or replace selected regions.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Background Swap
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a single image edit
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit this image with RunComfy. Keep the subject identity, pose, and clothing unchanged. Replace only the background with a clean studio setting.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Product Cleanup
prompt_templates.scenario: Consistent edits across several product images
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the best RunComfy route for a batch edit. Keep each product unchanged. Replace the backgrounds with light gray studio paper and keep the output square.

prompt_templates.title: Multilingual Text Replacement
prompt_templates.prompt: Use a RunComfy edit model for in-image text. Keep the photo, logo, layout, and colors unchanged. Replace only the headline with the provided translated text.

prompt_templates.title: Mask-Based Region Replacement
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced inpainting with source and mask URLs
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the RunComfy inpainting route. The white mask area is the only editable region. Replace that region with the requested object and preserve all unmasked details.

output_examples.input: A user asks to remove overhead cables from a street photo and provides a mask URL.
output_examples.output: The skill selects Z-Image Turbo Inpaint, uses the source image and mask, and describes strength settings for clean object removal.

output_examples.input: A user provides three product URLs and asks for a consistent square catalog set.
output_examples.output: The skill selects Nano Banana Edit, locks aspect ratio and resolution, and keeps product identity unchanged.

output_examples.input: A user needs a Japanese headline inserted into an existing poster.
output_examples.output: The skill selects GPT Image 2 Edit and focuses the prompt on exact text, script, layout, and brand preservation.

best_practices: Use non-sensitive image URLs and review privacy requirements before sending assets to RunComfy.
State what must stay unchanged before describing the requested edit.
Use masks for precise region edits and keep compound edits split into smaller passes.
anti_patterns: Do not send confidential media through public image URLs without approval.
Do not combine unrelated edits in one prompt when preservation matters.
Do not use the inpainting route without a clear grayscale mask URL.

faq.question: Does this skill edit images locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the local RunComfy CLI to submit jobs to RunComfy model services.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What image inputs are required?
faq.answer: The documented routes use publicly fetchable HTTPS image URLs. Inpainting also requires a mask URL.

faq.question: Can it process multiple images?
faq.answer: Yes. The Nano Banana route documents batch inputs up to 20 images.

faq.question: Can it replace text inside images?
faq.answer: Yes. The GPT Image 2 route is documented for multilingual in-image text replacement.

faq.question: What security warning should users see?
faq.answer: Users should know that prompts, image URLs, and generated outputs are processed through RunComfy services.
